info.description: This problem set focuses on fungal pathogens and antifungal agents. The difficulty may vary a lot through out the set, since this set contains a mix of four different cognitive levels of complexity (recall, understand, apply, analyze). The questions are already reviewed, so mistakes should have already been minimized, but if you come across one, you can report through the Forms in the "Info and Links".

q1.prompt: A laboratory is classifying various pathogenic fungi based on their morphology in clinical specimens. An organism is identified as a monomorphic mold with hyaline septate hyphae that lacks specific structures such as arthroconidia. Which of the following fits this description?

q2.prompt: A thermally dimorphic fungus is isolated from an HIV-positive patient in Southeast Asia. The organism is observed in tissue as small intracellular yeast-like cells that replicate by fission. Which of the following is the most likely causative agent?

q4.prompt: A patient with a history of neutrophil dysfunction, such as chronic granulomatous disease (NADPH oxidase deficiency), is at significantly increased risk for developing a specific invasive fungal infection. Which of the following organisms is most commonly associated with this specific immune defect?

q5.prompt: A patient being treated for invasive aspergillosis with a triazole antifungal agent reports experiencing transient visual disturbances, including seeing wavy lines and flashes of light. Which of the following medications is uniquely associated with these side effects?

q6.prompt: A 28-year-old male presents with a pruritic, circular, erythematous rash on his forearm that has a raised border and central clearing. A KOH preparation of skin scrapings reveals hyaline septate hyphae and barrel-shaped spores. Which of the following structures is most characteristic of dermatophytes when observed in clinical specimens?

q7.prompt: A 30-year-old veterinary worker from Bangkok develops a nodular lesion on her arm after handling an infected cat. She had received rabies post-exposure prophylaxis after a bite. Physical examination shows a solitary ulcerated nodule without lymphatic spread. A fungal infection is suspected. Which of the following laboratory characteristics best describes the causative organism?

options.label: Exists only as yeast at all temperatures
Exhibits thermal dimorphism, growing as mold at 25°C and yeast at 37°C
Forms nonseptate hyphae with broad branching

q8.prompt: A 24-year-old female presents with a thick, white, "cottage cheese-like" vaginal discharge and severe pruritus. Microscopic examination of the discharge shows budding yeasts and pseudohyphae. Which of the following is a recognized predisposing factor for this fungal infection?

options.label: Chronic use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
Poor personal hygiene in tropical climates

q9.prompt: A 45-year-old male undergoing chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukaemia develops fever and pleuritic chest pain during a period of profound neutropenia. A CT scan of the chest reveals several pulmonary nodules with surrounding ground-glass opacities. A biopsy of one of the nodules demonstrates branching, hyaline, septate hyphae that branch at 45-degree angles. Which of the following is the primary route of transmission for the pathogen responsible for this patient's condition?

q10.prompt: A patient is prescribed an oral azole antifungal, such as ketoconazole or itraconazole. The physician explains that these drugs should not be administered along with certain classes of medications. Co-administration with which of the following classes of medications would most likely decrease the absorption and efficacy of these azoles?
